source: Curated practitioner notes by Mohammed AlYahya. Verify critical decisions against official regulator publications.

entries.answer: The National Cybersecurity Authority (NCA) is Saudi Arabia's central cybersecurity regulator, established in 2017. It publishes the national baseline controls every public-sector and critical-infrastructure entity must implement, and it has inspection authority. Its main control sets are ECC (baseline), CCC (critical systems), CSCC (cloud), DCC (data), TCC (telework), and OSMACC (organizational social media). Ask me about any of them by name.

SDAIA (Saudi Data and AI Authority) is the national authority for data and AI policy, and the enforcement body for PDPL. Where NCA owns the cybersecurity baseline, SDAIA owns the data/privacy baseline. They overlap on things like data classification — which is why I read NCA DCC and PDPL together, not separately, when advising clients.

SAMA (Saudi Central Bank, formerly Saudi Arabian Monetary Authority) is the regulator for all banks, insurance companies, and financing companies in Saudi Arabia. It publishes two of the most important financial-sector frameworks: the Cyber Security Framework (CSF) and the IT Governance Framework (ITGF). Ask me about either by name.

CST (Communications, Space & Technology Commission — formerly CITC, the Communications and IT Commission) is the telecom and ICT-sector regulator. It publishes the Cybersecurity Regulatory Framework (CRF) for service providers in the ICT sector. If your business touches telecom, ISP, hosting, or regulated digital services, you will likely owe CST on top of whatever NCA baseline applies.

NCA ECC — Essential Cybersecurity Controls, issued 2018. The baseline for every in-scope public-sector and critical-infrastructure entity in Saudi Arabia. Built around 5 main domains, 29 subdomains, and 114 controls, covering governance, defense, resilience, third-party/cloud, and ICS. Mandatory for in-scope entities. Read it as a floor, not a ceiling: passing ECC means you have the baseline; it does not mean you are mature. I almost always pair ECC scoping with ISO 27001 evidence rigor.

NCA ECC is structured around 5 main domains, 29 subdomains, and 114 controls. The five main domains are: (1) Cybersecurity Governance — strategy, policies, roles, risk management, compliance, audit. (2) Cybersecurity Defense — this is where network security, asset management, identity and access, data protection, cryptography, vulnerability management, endpoint and email security, secure configuration, and event logging all live. (3) Cybersecurity Resilience — business continuity and disaster recovery from a cyber angle. (4) Third-Party and Cloud Computing Cybersecurity — supplier risk and cloud adoption controls. (5) Industrial Control Systems Cybersecurity — for OT/ICS environments. Practitioner note: most of what people ask about — network security, IAM, data protection, logging — sits inside Domain 2 (Cybersecurity Defense). I do not have the individual control IDs in my corpus; pull the official NCA ECC document for that level of detail.

NCA CCC — Critical Systems Cybersecurity Controls, issued 2019. Layered on top of ECC for critical systems and critical national infrastructure (energy, water, finance, health, telecom, transport, government services). Stricter than ECC: expects segmentation, stronger monitoring, and zero-trust-style assumptions for critical-system boundaries. Rule of thumb: if you are in scope of CCC, you are also in scope of ECC — treat CCC as the ceiling and ECC as the floor, and build one unified control set that satisfies both.

NCA CSCC — Cloud Cybersecurity Controls, issued 2020. Applies to both Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) operating in Saudi Arabia and to cloud tenants. Tiered by data classification and service sensitivity — the higher the classification of data you put in the cloud, the tighter the control set. Two practical implications: (1) data localization constraints for sensitive classes, (2) a formal cloud risk assessment before adoption, not after. I usually scope CSCC against the data classification from NCA DCC — they are designed to be read together.

NCA DCC — Data Cybersecurity Controls. Focused on data classification and protection across the data lifecycle. Uses a four-tier classification scheme (roughly: Top Secret, Secret, Confidential, Public), and maps protection requirements to each tier. Practical order I recommend: do the DCC classification exercise before touching CSCC or PDPL — because both of those are downstream of knowing which data you actually hold at which sensitivity. DCC is where most programs should start if they have never done a data inventory.

NCA TCC — Telework Cybersecurity Controls. Created to formalize expectations around secure remote work: endpoint hygiene, secure connectivity (VPN/ZTNA), session protection, and identity verification for off-network access. In practice I treat TCC as a subset of ECC rather than a new program — almost everything it asks for can be satisfied by a well-designed access-control and endpoint-management program. Where it matters most is documenting those controls explicitly so a regulator can see the telework use case was considered.

NCA OSMACC — Organizations' Social Media Accounts Cybersecurity Controls. A narrow, targeted control set covering account takeover protection, MFA on official accounts, content approval workflows, access lifecycle for social-media admins, and incident response for compromised accounts. Looks small until you remember how many national-brand takeover incidents have started with a reused password on a shared corporate X/Instagram account. I treat OSMACC as a one-afternoon compliance exercise for mature organizations — it should not eat program capacity, but it should not be skipped.

NCA SCyWF — Saudi Cybersecurity Workforce Framework. Role/skill taxonomy for cybersecurity jobs in Saudi Arabia, mapped loosely to the US NICE framework. Used for workforce planning, role definitions, and training pathways. Useful if you are building a security team structure or justifying headcount to HR and finance; less relevant for day-to-day control operations. I reach for it when a client asks "what should the org chart look like?" not when they ask "what should the controls do?"

SAMA CSF — Cyber Security Framework, issued 2017 (v1.0). The cybersecurity-specific framework for all SAMA-regulated entities (banks, insurance, financing companies). Structured around four main domains — cybersecurity leadership and governance, cyber risk management and compliance, cyber operations and technology, and third-party cybersecurity — with a 4-level maturity scale. CSF is narrower and sharper than ITGF: ITGF is about IT governance generally; CSF is about cyber posture specifically. A well-run bank treats them as one integrated program, not two separate projects.

SAMA ITGF — IT Governance Framework. The IT governance framework all SAMA-regulated banks and financial institutions must comply with. Expects: a 3 lines of defence model, board-level IT risk ownership, KRIs and KPIs tied to real decisions, and inspection-readiness as an ongoing posture, not a point-in-time exercise. Read it as a decision framework, not a checklist — SAMA is asking whether your decisions are defensible, not whether you have the controls. I have reduced critical audit findings by ~35% at a Tier-1 bank by applying exactly that reading.

SAMA BCM Framework — Business Continuity Management framework for SAMA-regulated entities. Covers business impact analysis, recovery strategies, crisis management, and testing. I treat it as operational continuity discipline, not a cybersecurity document — but it overlaps heavily with cyber resilience, especially for ransomware scenarios. A bank that has done BCM properly already has most of the artifacts cyber resilience needs. Note: always verify current BCM framework version against SAMA's latest circulars before implementation.

CST CRF — Cybersecurity Regulatory Framework, issued by CST (formerly CITC). The sector-specific cybersecurity framework for telecom operators, ISPs, hosting providers, and other regulated ICT service providers in Saudi Arabia. Structured around domains similar to NCA ECC but with ICT-sector-specific expectations on network integrity, subscriber data, lawful interception, and service availability. If you are in ICT, you owe CST CRF in addition to NCA baseline — treat them as overlapping, not either/or.

Saudi Aramco SACS-002 is Aramco's Third Party Cybersecurity Standard. Any contractor or supplier connecting to Aramco systems or handling Aramco data must obtain a Cybersecurity Compliance Certificate (CCC) — not to be confused with NCA's CCC (Critical Systems Controls). Aramco CCC is tiered by contractor type and connectivity profile: different tiers (commonly described as Gold/Silver/Bronze-style levels) apply depending on whether you are on-site, remotely connected, or handling sensitive data. Practical reality: for most contractors this is a 3–6 month engagement with an accredited auditor, and it is the single most common "we cannot start the contract until…" blocker I see in the energy sector.

PDPL — Personal Data Protection Law, enforced by SDAIA. Governs how personal data is collected, processed, stored, and transferred. Key obligations: lawful basis for every processing activity, data subject rights (access, correction, deletion, objection), breach notification, and constraints on cross-border transfer. PDPL has extraterritorial effect — it can reach foreign companies processing Saudi residents' data. I treat PDPL as a data problem with a control wrapper. Solve the data problem first — discovery, ownership, lawful basis — and the controls tell you what they need to be. Most failures I see are ownership gaps, not control gaps.

ISO/IEC 27001 — the international standard for Information Security Management Systems (ISMS). Risk-based, certifiable, globally recognized. Strength: rigor on evidence and the management-system loop (Plan-Do-Check-Act). Weakness in a Saudi context: it is not country-specific and does not satisfy NCA or SAMA on its own. My rule: build to ISO's evidence standard, scope to NCA's mandatory controls. That single sentence has saved clients months of duplicate documentation work.

NCA ECC vs ISO 27001 — short version: ~80% control overlap. NCA is more prescriptive about who owns what and Saudi-specific obligations (data localization, national CIP). ISO is more flexible on the "how" but more rigorous on show me the evidence. Build to ISO's evidence standard, scope to NCA's mandatory controls. Order matters — building NCA-first usually produces documentation too thin for ISO and forces rework. Exception: if a regulator inspection is imminent, scope-and-close NCA first; ISO can wait.

NCA ECC vs NCA CCC: ECC is the baseline every in-scope entity must meet. CCC is a layered, stricter set for critical systems and critical national infrastructure. If you are in scope of CCC, you are automatically in scope of ECC — treat CCC as the ceiling and ECC as the floor. Build one unified control catalog that satisfies both at once, tagged per control which regime requires it. Running them as two separate projects is how programs waste six months.

SAMA vs NCA: different audiences. SAMA (CSF + ITGF) is financial-sector-specific, with deeper expectations on three-lines-of-defence, KRI escalation, and board-level risk ownership. NCA ECC is the broader Saudi baseline for government and critical-sector entities. If you are a Saudi bank, SAMA wins on hierarchy — but you will still satisfy ECC as a side effect of doing SAMA properly. If you are a non-bank in scope of ECC, you are not under SAMA.

PDPL vs NCA DCC: they look similar but target different concerns. PDPL is about personal data — rights of the data subject, lawful basis, cross-border transfer. NCA DCC is about all organizational data — classification, protection, lifecycle. Where they overlap is classification: PDPL creates categories of personal data (personal, sensitive), DCC creates organizational classification tiers (Top Secret → Public). Run one data discovery exercise that outputs both classifications in one pass — never do them twice.

Aramco SACS-002 vs NCA ECC: SACS-002 is a private-sector procurement requirement, not a national regulation. NCA ECC is a national regulatory baseline. A contractor working for Aramco needs the Aramco CCC certificate and — if they also handle government or critical-infrastructure scope — NCA compliance. They do not substitute for each other. In practice I see contractors treat Aramco CCC as the binding requirement because it is the blocker to revenue, and layer NCA where applicable.

Start with three questions: (1) Which regulator can inspect you in the next 12 months? Build to satisfy them first. (2) What data do you actually hold and where? No control program survives bad data discovery. (3) Who owns risk decisions? If the answer is "the security team," you have a governance gap before you have a control gap. Once you have those three, the framework choice almost picks itself.

Maturity in NCA / SAMA contexts is typically a 1–5 scale (Initial → Optimized). My rule: target Level 3 (Defined and consistently applied) across the program before chasing Level 4–5 anywhere. Uneven maturity (5 in one domain, 2 in another) is what regulators flag, not absolute level. Pick your weakest domain and pull it up before optimizing your strongest.

Audit findings are usually capture problems, not control problems. If your risk register is filled in by people who do not trust it, you will close findings and grow new ones at the same rate. The fix is upstream: make the act of capturing risk produce useful inputs (asset classification, residual scoring, KRI links, ownership at the right level). I have used this approach to reduce critical findings ~35% in 6 months at a Tier-1 bank, without a tool change.

Third-party cybersecurity shows up in every Saudi framework: NCA ECC has a third-party domain, SAMA CSF has its own third-party section, Aramco SACS-002 is entirely about contractors, and PDPL imposes processor/sub-processor obligations. One practical rule: build a single third-party risk register keyed by supplier, and tag each supplier with which regimes apply. Running a separate vendor program per regulator is the most common source of duplicated work I see.

English: I am the GRC Assistant built by Mohammed AlYahya. I answer questions on Saudi cybersecurity and data frameworks — NCA, SAMA, CST, Aramco SACS-002, PDPL, and ISO 27001 — grounded in his own curated practitioner notes.

العربية: أنا مساعد الحوكمة والمخاطر والامتثال الخاص بـمحمد اليحيا. أجيب على الأسئلة المتعلقة بأطر الأمن السيبراني وحماية البيانات في المملكة العربية السعودية — NCA وSAMA وCST ومعيار أرامكو SACS-002 ونظام حماية البيانات الشخصية PDPL وأيزو 27001 — اعتماداً على ملاحظاته العملية المختارة.

I can answer questions on the main Saudi cybersecurity and data regimes:

NCA — ECC, CCC, CSCC, DCC, TCC, OSMACC, SCyWF
SAMA — CSF, ITGF, BCM
CST — CRF
Saudi Aramco — SACS-002 / CCC
SDAIA — PDPL
International — ISO 27001

I can also compare any two of these, and explain where to start a program, how to read maturity levels, and how to handle third-party cybersecurity across all of them. Ask by name.
